_work.title: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Ottoman, Persian, Mughal and Arab lands
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The Islamic science of letters (ʿilm al-ḥurūf) treated the Arabic alphabet as the substance of creation and a key to hidden power: each letter carries a numerical value (abjad), a planetary and elemental correspondence, and a place in the divine Names. This collection gathers sacred calligraphy as occult diagram — the budūḥ and wafq magic squares of al-Būnī's lineage, zoomorphic calligrams of ʿAlī as lion and the bismillah as a hoopoe, the mirror (muthanna) names of the Five Holy Ones, the Hurufi and Bektashi face-as-Quran mysticism, the muqaṭṭaʿāt 'mysterious letters', the hilya portrait-in-words of the Prophet, the imperial tughra, and the Ottoman levha panels in which a single sacred phrase becomes a talismanic image.

entries.title: Calligram in the Shape of a Lion (ʿAlī)
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Vesal Shirazi (attrib.)
entries.summary:
A zoomorphic calligram in which a devotional Arabic phrase honouring **ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib** is bent into the body of a lion.

## Iconography
The whole beast — mane, paw, curling tail — is formed of cursive script, the letters doubling as anatomy. The lion is the standard emblem of ʿAlī, the *Asad Allāh* ('Lion of God'), so the image is both portrait and prayer: the holy name *becomes* the holy animal.

## Significance
Zoomorphic calligraphy belongs to the lettrist conviction that the [[motif:sacred letters|sacred letters]] are living, generative substance. By shaping a venerated name into a lion the calligrapher makes a talisman that is read and seen at once — a practice especially developed in Shiʿi and dervish circles. Such pieces circulated as protective images in 18th–19th-century Iran and India.

## For artists & game designers
A template for 'word-creatures' — sigils whose silhouette spells the spell they cast.

entries.title: Shiʿi Calligraphy of ʿAlī as the Tiger of God
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (modern redrawing)
entries.summary:
A black-and-white zoomorphic calligram forming a striding big cat (lion/tiger) out of an Arabic invocation of **ʿAlī**.

## Iconography
Interlaced cursive letters compose the head, flank and legs of the animal; the negative spaces between strokes give the impression of dappled fur. The text is a devotional formula naming ʿAlī, the 'Lion/Tiger of God' (*Ḥaydar*).

## Significance
The piece restates the lettrist principle that a holy name can be re-bodied as its emblematic creature. As a clean line drawing it shows the underlying scaffolding of such calligrams better than gilded album leaves, making the letter-to-limb correspondences legible. It belongs to the broad family of ʿAlī-as-lion talismans tied to the *Nād-i ʿAlī* prayer.

## For artists & game designers
Useful reference sheet: the bare linework reveals how to read a creature back into its constituent glyphs.

entries.title: Nād-i ʿAlī Prayer Cut in the Shape of a Lion
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Mīr ʿAlī Heravī (attrib.)
entries.summary:
An album page on which the **Nād-i ʿAlī** — a celebrated prayer of intercession to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib — is rendered as découpage (cut-paper) calligraphy in the form of a lion.

## Iconography
The entire lion, mounted on a contrasting ground, is built from the cut-out words of the prayer; the medium of paper-cutting turns the text into a solid silhouette. The lion again signals ʿAlī, the *Asad Allāh*.

## Significance
The *Nād-i ʿAlī* ("Call upon ʿAlī, the manifester of wonders") was prized as a protective and talismanic invocation across the Persianate world. Shaping it into the lion fuses the apotropaic power of the words with the iconic force of the image — a high-art instance of letter-mysticism in service of devotion. The leaf is associated with the great calligrapher Mīr ʿAlī Heravī.

## For artists & game designers
Découpage as game-asset: a talisman where the prayer text literally is the relic's silhouette.

entries.title: Bismillah in the Shape of a Hoopoe
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
The **basmala** — *bismillāh ar-raḥmān ar-raḥīm*, 'In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful' — written so that its letters form a **hoopoe**.

## Iconography
A crested hoopoe, gilded and finely scrolled, is composed entirely of the words of the opening invocation: the crest, beak, wing and tail are each carried by a stroke of the phrase. Black calligraphy on a gold ground.

## Significance
The hoopoe (*hudhud*) is the messenger-bird of Solomon and, in ʿAṭṭār's *Conference of the Birds*, the guide of the soul's journey to the divine — a fitting body for the sacred name that opens every act and every Quranic chapter. Bird-calligrams of the [[motif:bismillah|bismillah]] are a favourite of the lettrist tradition, where the formula's letters are also counted and meditated upon for their abjad value.

## For artists & game designers
A model bismillah-bird: a single holy phrase that resolves into a guide-creature.

entries.title: The Buraq Worshipped by Two Princes
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA)
entries.summary:
A painting of the **Buraq**, the winged human-faced steed that carried the Prophet on the Night Journey (miʿrāj), here venerated by two kneeling princes; the verso carries calligraphy.

## Iconography
The Buraq, with a woman's face, peacock tail and equine body, is shown crowned and haloed. The pairing of image (recto) and sacred script (verso) is typical of the talismanic album leaf, where the holy beast and the holy text guard one another.

## Significance
The Buraq is itself a frequent subject of zoomorphic calligrams (the bismillah-as-Buraq), and the creature's body is treated, like the lion of ʿAlī, as a vessel for sacred text. This LACMA leaf shows the painted source-image from which the calligraphic Buraq abstracts. It belongs to the devotional, protective image-culture of [[motif:miʿraj|the Night Journey]].

## For artists & game designers
A fully designed mythic mount — human face, peacock tail, equine body, crowned — ready as a steed asset.

citations.text: Christiane Gruber, *The Praiseworthy One: The Prophet Muhammad in Islamic Texts and Images* (2018).

entries.title: Hilye-i Şerif (Calligraphic Description of the Prophet)
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ottoman)
entries.summary:
A **hilye** (Arabic *ḥilya*) — the formalised calligraphic 'portrait in words' describing the physical and moral qualities of the Prophet Muhammad.

## Iconography
The genre has a fixed architecture: a top panel (*başmakam*) with the basmala, a central medallion (*göbek*) holding the descriptive text, four corner roundels for the Rightly-Guided Caliphs, and a lower panel with a closing verse — all bordered like an icon. No face is depicted; the description *is* the likeness.

## Significance
The hilye answers the prohibition on figural images of the Prophet by substituting a verbal-visual diagram believed to carry blessing (*baraka*); to keep or gaze upon one was held to confer protection. As a sacred design made wholly of words and ordered space, it is a paradigm of lettrist devotion. The form was codified by the Ottoman master Hâfız Osman in the 17th century.

## For artists & game designers
A strict template UI: fixed panels and medallions you can re-skin as a 'relic-card' frame.

entries.title: Hilye by Hâfız Osman
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Hâfız Osman
entries.summary:
A **hilye** by **Hâfız Osman** (1642–1698), the Ottoman calligrapher who standardised the genre's classic layout, now in the Sadberk Hanım Museum.

## Iconography
The sheet follows the canonical scheme: basmala header, a circular central medallion containing the description of the Prophet, four caliphal roundels, and a framing border, executed in disciplined thuluth and naskh.

## Significance
Hâfız Osman's design fixed the hilye as a devotional and quasi-talismanic object reproduced for centuries; owning one was thought to bring blessing and protection. Its geometry — a sacred text organised into concentric and cornered fields — makes the page a [[motif:devotional diagram|devotional diagram]] as much as a piece of writing, the lettrist ideal of word-as-icon.

## For artists & game designers
The authoritative reference for the hilye frame: copy its proportions for an in-game holy-relic document.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

entries.title: Tughra of Sultan Süleyman the Magnificent
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Ottoman chancery (nişancı)
entries.summary:
The illuminated **tughra** (sovereign monogram) of **Süleyman the Magnificent**, the calligraphic cipher that authenticated every Ottoman imperial decree.

## Iconography
The tughra weaves the sultan's name and titles into a fixed abstract form: a cluster of vertical staves (*tuğ*) on the right, three looping ovals (*beyze*) on the left, and a flourish sweeping outward, here filled with gold floral illumination.

## Significance
More than a signature, the tughra functioned as a quasi-magical sign of presence and authority: to bear it was to bear the sultan's word made visible, and its inimitable design protected documents like a [[motif:seal|seal]] or talisman against forgery. As a sacred-name-form abstracted into pure pattern, it sits at the meeting of calligraphy, heraldry and lettrist sign-magic.

## For artists & game designers
A ready 'royal seal' generator: a personal name compressed into an unforgeable emblem of power.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

entries.title: Calligraphic Composition (Levha) by Hasan Rıza Şumnu
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Hasan Rıza Şumnu
entries.summary:
An Ottoman **levha** — a single-sheet calligraphic panel made to be framed and hung — signed by Hasan Rıza Şumnu and dated 1886–87.

## Iconography
A sacred phrase is laid out as a balanced symmetrical composition in bold thuluth, with the words interlocking to fill the field as a designed image rather than a line of running text.

## Significance
The levha brought monumental sacred calligraphy out of the mosque and into the home as a devotional and protective wall-image. A well-made levha of a Quranic verse or holy name was understood to carry blessing into the room it guarded — the everyday face of [[motif:sacred letters|letter-mysticism]], where a phrase becomes a talismanic object simply by being beautifully and deliberately composed.

## For artists & game designers
The 'framed inscription' as environmental prop — a hangable holy panel for interior set-dressing.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

entries.title: Square Kufic Bismillah Page by Ahmed Karahisari
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Ahmed Karahisari
entries.summary:
A calligraphic page attributed to the great Ottoman master **Ahmed Karahisari** (d. 1556), combining a cursive **bismillah** with two geometric panels in **square (bannāʾī) Kufic**.

## Iconography
At centre a flowing bismillah; above and below, sacred phrases are folded into diamond grids of square Kufic, where each letter is squared off and woven into an interlocking maze that must be 'read' by tracing the path of the strokes.

## Significance
Square Kufic turns sacred text into pure geometry, the closest the Arabic script comes to a [[motif:magic square|magic square]]: the eye decodes a word from a lattice. Karahisari, founder of an influential Ottoman school, exploited this to make text legible as architecture and ornament. Such grid-letters were used on buildings and as talismanic medallions, sitting squarely within the lettrist treatment of writing as occult diagram.

## For artists & game designers
Square Kufic is a built-in 'glyph puzzle' mechanic — a word hidden in a grid the player must trace.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

entries.title: Leaf from an Ottoman Calligraphy Diploma
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Muhammad Hilmi Afandi
entries.summary:
An illuminated leaf from an Ottoman **icazet** (calligraphy licence/diploma) by Muhammad Hilmi Afandi, in the Walters Art Museum.

## Iconography
The gilt-framed page shows model lines of sacred text in alternating thuluth and naskh, with an illuminated header and floral panels — the showcase by which a master certified a pupil's mastery of the holy script.

## Significance
The icazet embodies the chain of transmission (*silsila*) by which the correct, blessed forms of the sacred letters passed master-to-pupil — a quasi-initiatic lineage that treats calligraphy as a spiritual discipline, not mere penmanship. This is the institutional backbone of the lettrist culture in which writing the [[motif:sacred letters|holy letters]] is itself an act of devotion and power.

## For artists & game designers
A 'mastery certificate' artifact — proof of a craft-lineage the player can earn or forge.

citations.text: M. Uğur Derman, *Letters in Gold: Ottoman Calligraphy* (1998).

entries.title: The Mysterious Letters Ṭā-Hā (Surah 20)
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (modern roundel)
entries.summary:
A roundel displaying **Ṭā-Hā** (طه), the two disconnected 'mysterious letters' (*muqaṭṭaʿāt*) that open the 20th sura of the Quran.

## Iconography
Within a green medallion the word *sūra* and the number 20 frame the large isolated letters Ṭā and Hā, written without the surrounding text — exactly as they stand, enigmatic, at the head of the chapter.

## Significance
Twenty-nine Quranic suras begin with such [[motif:muqattaat|muqaṭṭaʿāt]] — single or grouped letters (Alif-Lām-Mīm, Yā-Sīn, Ṭā-Hā) whose meaning is undisclosed. For the lettrists they were the supreme proof that bare letters carry hidden power: commentators computed their abjad values, sought their secret names of God, and treated them as keys to the cosmos. They are the Quranic charter of the science of letters.

## For artists & game designers
The muqaṭṭaʿāt are canonical 'untranslatable runes' — sacred glyphs whose meaning is a deliberate mystery.

entries.title: The Budūḥ Magic Square
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: after Zakariyā al-Qazwīnī
entries.summary:
The **budūḥ** — the classic 3×3 magic square of Islamic letter-magic, here in a manuscript hand associated with the cosmographer al-Qazwīnī.

## Iconography
Nine cells hold the Arabic numerals 2-9-4 / 7-5-3 / 6-1-8 (or their letter equivalents), every row, column and diagonal summing to fifteen. The four corner letters bā-dāl-wāw-ḥā spell the talismanic word *BUDŪḤ*.

## Significance
The 3×3 square (the *wafq* of Saturn, the Chinese Lo Shu) entered Islamic magic as the [[motif:buduh|budūḥ]], invoked as a guardian word on amulets for safe travel, childbirth and protection — its mere name a charm. It is the foundational object where [[motif:abjad numerology|abjad numerology]] and the magic square meet, and it passed into Latin and Hebrew magic alike.

## For artists & game designers
The budūḥ is a portable, drawable ward: a 3×3 grid the player inscribes to seal a door or a journey.

citations.text: Edgar W. Lane / E. Doutté on the budūḥ; J. Sesiano, *Magic Squares in Islam* (2017).

entries.title: Persian Wafq — a 6×6 Magic Square with Instructions
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A 16th-century Persian manuscript page giving a **wafq** (magic square) of order six, with the construction rules written out above and below the grid.

## Iconography
A 6×6 grid is filled with the numbers 1–36 so that every row and column shares a constant sum (111); the surrounding Persian text explains, cell by cell, how the figure is to be built — a working recipe, not just a finished talisman.

## Significance
The order-six square is the *wafq* of the Sun in the planetary series, and its construction was a prized branch of the lettrist mathematical sciences (*ʿilm al-awfāq*). Squares like this were engraved on amulets and astral talismans, their numbers tied through [[motif:abjad numerology|abjad]] to divine names and planetary powers. The inclusion of the method shows the practitioner's craft, descending from al-Būnī and the Brethren of Purity.

## For artists & game designers
A genuine in-world 'spell recipe': the grid plus the step-by-step text for building it.

citations.text: Jacques Sesiano, *Magic Squares in the Tenth Century* / *Les carrés magiques dans les pays islamiques* (2004).

entries.title: Calligraphic Roundel of the Name of Aḥmad al-Būnī
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (modern roundel)
entries.summary:
A calligraphic medallion spelling the name and honorific of **al-Shaykh Aḥmad b. ʿAlī al-Būnī** (d. c. 1225), the foundational author of the Islamic science of letters.

## Iconography
White cursive script on black fills a double-ruled roundel: 'the Shaykh Aḥmad ibn ʿAlī al-Būnī, may God have mercy on him', composed as a balanced seal-like emblem.

## Significance
Al-Būnī is the towering figure of lettrist magic, credited with the *Shams al-maʿārif al-kubrā* ('Sun of Great Knowledge'), the encyclopaedia of the [[motif:names of god|divine Names]], magic squares, and the occult properties of the Arabic letters that stands behind centuries of Islamic and, via translation, European talismanic practice. Rendering his very name as a sealed roundel honours him in the medium he theorised.

## For artists & game designers
A named 'grimoire author' emblem — the sigil of a magus the player can quest to study under.

citations.text: Noah Gardiner, 'Forbidden Knowledge? Notes on the Production and Reception of the Works of al-Būnī' (2012).

entries.title: The Ninety-Nine Beautiful Names of God
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A golden calligraphy board displaying **al-asmāʾ al-ḥusnā**, the ninety-nine Most Beautiful Names of God, each set in its own cartouche.

## Iconography
The Names — al-Raḥmān, al-Raḥīm, al-Malik, and so on — radiate around a central medallion (typically the Name *Allāh*), the whole arranged as an ordered grid or rosette of letter-talismans.

## Significance
The ninety-nine [[motif:names of god|Names]] are the heart of lettrist devotion and magic: each Name has an abjad value, a planetary and elemental correspondence, and a prescribed number of repetitions (*dhikr*) thought to release its specific power. Al-Būnī's *Shams al-maʿārif* tabulates their squares and seals. Displayed together they form a complete map of the divine attributes — a master-diagram of sacred letters.

## For artists & game designers
A 99-entry 'attribute system': each Name a distinct power with its own number, sound and correspondence.

entries.title: Panjtan — The Five Holy Ones in Calligraphy
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Kānūn-i Hunar-i Shīʿī (Shiʿi art workshop)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (Kānūn-i Hunar-i Shīʿī). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A calligraphic composition of the **Panjtan-i Āl-i ʿAbā** — the Five Holy Ones: Muhammad, ʿAlī, Fāṭima, Ḥasan and Ḥusayn — woven into a single symmetrical design.

## Iconography
The five sacred names are arranged as an interlaced star or rosette, often in mirror (muthanna) symmetry so the names reflect one another around a central axis. No faces appear; the persons are present purely as their names.

## Significance
In Shiʿi devotion the Panjtan are the locus of divine blessing and protection, and their five names are deployed together as a powerful talismanic formula — inscribed on amulets, banners and the hand-shaped *panja*. As a sacred-name [[motif:mirror calligraphy|mirror-calligram]], the piece exemplifies the lettrist principle that arranging holy names in ordered, symmetrical form concentrates their power.

## For artists & game designers
A five-fold 'patron set' — a single emblem binding five named protectors into one ward.

entries.title: Mirror Image of 'ʿAlī walī Allāh'
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Ibrāhīm Maḥmūd
entries.summary:
A **muthannā** (mirror) calligraphic panel of the Shiʿi testimony *ʿAlī walī Allāh* ('ʿAlī is the friend/vicegerent of God'), the phrase doubled in perfect reflection.

## Iconography
The inscription is written once and then mirrored across a vertical axis, so the two halves meet like a butterfly's wings; the symmetry makes a single phrase read as a balanced ornamental whole.

## Significance
Mirror calligraphy (*muthannā* or *aynalı*) was prized exactly because the doubling intensifies a sacred phrase into a self-contained, talismanic image. Applied to *ʿAlī walī Allāh* — the distinctive Shiʿi addition to the call to prayer — it produces a devotional sign carrying both confessional identity and protective power, a paradigm case of the lettrist transformation of a [[motif:sacred names|holy phrase]] into diagram.

## For artists & game designers
The mirror technique is a free 'sigil-doubler': reflect any inscription to make it read as an emblem.

entries.title: Bismillah in Calligraphy
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Laisurw2020, Wikimedia)
entries.summary:
The **bismillah** — *bismillāh ar-raḥmān ar-raḥīm* — written as a single flowing line of display calligraphy.

## Iconography
The nineteen letters of the formula are drawn out in expansive thuluth, the elongated *sīn* of *bismi* sweeping under the whole phrase as a base-line that gathers the words into one gesture.

## Significance
The basmala opens every chapter of the Quran but one and every blessed act; its nineteen letters became a focus of lettrist numerology (the 'mystery of nineteen'), and the phrase itself is the most common subject of zoomorphic calligrams and talismanic panels. As the door-formula of speech and writing alike, it is the natural seed of the whole [[motif:sacred letters|science of letters]].

## For artists & game designers
The universal 'invocation header' — the phrase that opens every spell, document and journey.

entries.title: Bismillah Roundel (Mirror Composition)
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (vector, Wikimedia 'Frater5')
entries.summary:
The **bismillah** composed into a circular seal-like medallion, the words folded to fill a roundel.

## Iconography
Black on white, the formula is arranged so its strokes radiate and interlock within a circle, producing a compact emblem rather than a running line — close to a stamp or seal.

## Significance
Compressing the basmala into a roundel is the move that turns a phrase into a [[motif:seal|seal]]: a self-contained sign that can be impressed, worn or hung as a unit of blessing and protection. The circular format aligns the sacred opening-formula with the talismanic logic of the magic-square amulet and the tughra, where holy text becomes a closed, portable device.

## For artists & game designers
A drop-in 'holy stamp' icon — the bismillah as a seal you can emboss on doors, scrolls and wax.

entries.title: Basmala (Naskh)
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Saddam19, Wikimedia)
entries.summary:
A clean single-line rendering of the **basmala** in a clear naskh hand.

## Iconography
The phrase *bismillāh ar-raḥmān ar-raḥīm* is set out plainly, letter by letter, so the individual graphemes — and thus their abjad values — are easy to count and contemplate.

## Significance
Where display calligraphy dramatises the formula, a plain naskh basmala is the version the lettrist actually meditates on and computes: each letter weighed for its [[motif:abjad|abjad]] number, the totals sought in the divine Names. The legibility makes this a useful reference for the very alphabet on which the whole science of letters operates.

## For artists & game designers
A legible 'reference inscription' — the base text from which stylised and zoomorphic variants derive.

entries.title: The Name of God (Allāh)
entries.work: Lettrism — The Esoteric Science of Letters (ʿIlm al-Ḥurūf)
entries.creator: Anonymous (vector, Wikimedia 'Mr100percent')
entries.summary:
The single word **Allāh** — the Greatest Name (*al-ism al-aʿẓam*) — rendered in bold green calligraphy.

## Iconography
The name is built from the doubled *lām* rising as twin verticals, the looping *hāʾ* below, the *alif* and the *shadda* above — a tight, instantly recognisable monogram of four written elements.

## Significance
For the lettrists the Name *Allāh* is the seed of all the others and the supreme object of meditation; its letters (alif-lām-lām-hā) were analysed for their successive disappearance down to the bare *hū* ('He'), and its abjad value computed as a key to the divine. As the [[motif:names of god|Greatest Name]], it is held to be the most powerful of all letter-talismans, the centre around which the ninety-nine Names and every magic square are organised.

## For artists & game designers
The ultimate 'true name' token — the single glyph at the centre of every ward and rosette in the set.
